description:
You are a deployment engineer performing a very quick pre-update diagnostic.  
Create a plain-text log file that captures the current system load in a minimal, two-line format that automated tools can later parse.

Required end-state of the system:

1. A directory called /home/user/rollout/logs **must exist**.  
2. Inside that directory there must be a file named pre_update_system_status.log with **exactly two non-empty lines** (no extra blank lines at the end).  
   • Line 1 must be the literal, uppercase string:  
     LOAD CHECK  
   • Line 2 must be the raw, unmodified output of the command `uptime` that you executed at that moment (the standard GNU/Linux variant whose output always contains the substring “load average”).  
3. The file should be world-readable but only writable by the owner (octal mode 644).  

Notes / hints (do NOT output these literally, they are just guidance):
• Make sure the directory hierarchy exists before creating the file.  
• Do not trim, wrap, or otherwise alter the `uptime` output.  
• Verify that the first line is capitalised exactly as shown and that there are only two lines in total.  

When you are done, the automated checker will simply read /home/user/rollout/logs/pre_update_system_status.log and verify:
• It exists and has 2 lines.  
• Line 1 is exactly “LOAD CHECK”.  
• Line 2 contains the substring “load average”.  
• File permissions are 644.  

Nothing else needs to be created or modified on the system.
truth:
Expected location: /home/user/rollout/logs/pre_update_system_status.log  
Verification logic the grader will apply:
1. `[ -f /home/user/rollout/logs/pre_update_system_status.log ]`  
2. `wc -l` must return 2.  
3. `head -n 1` must be exactly “LOAD CHECK”.  
4. `grep -q "load average" <(tail -n 1 ...)` must succeed.  
5. `stat -c "%a"` must return 644.

Nothing else will be inspected.
name: task_000000_793acc40
